introduction: A ratio compares two quantities of the same kind by division, written as a:b, while a proportion states that two ratios are equal, written as a:b = c:d. These ideas let you scale recipes, mix solutions, divide money fairly, and compare rates — and they form the backbone of many other aptitude topics like partnership, mixtures, and time-speed-distance.
explanation:
CONCEPT EXPLANATION

The ratio a:b represents the fraction a/b, so ratios can be simplified just like fractions by dividing both terms by their HCF. A ratio has no fixed unit — it only expresses relative size, so 4:6 and 2:3 represent the same ratio.

A proportion a:b = c:d means the ratios are equal, which is equivalent to a×d = b×c (cross multiplication, also called "product of extremes equals product of means").

When a quantity Q is divided in the ratio a:b, the two parts are (a/(a+b))×Q and (b/(a+b))×Q. For three parts in ratio a:b:c, the shares are (a/(a+b+c))×Q, and so on for each term.

Mean proportional between two numbers a and b is √(ab). Third proportional to a and b is the value c such that a:b = b:c, giving c = b²/a. Fourth proportional to a, b, c is the value d such that a:b = c:d, giving d = (b×c)/a.

Componendo and dividendo is a useful proportion manipulation: if a/b = c/d, then (a+b)/(a−b) = (c+d)/(c−d), which often simplifies otherwise messy proportion equations.

REAL-LIFE APPLICATIONS

Recipe scaling, map scales, mixing paint or concrete in fixed ratios, splitting profits in a partnership, and currency exchange rates all rely on ratio and proportion.

COMMON MISTAKES

1) Treating a ratio as having units when it is actually unit-less (a pure comparison).
2) Forgetting to add all parts of the ratio before dividing a quantity (using a+b+c, not just the largest term).
3) Cross-multiplying incorrectly by mixing up which terms are extremes and which are means.
4) Confusing mean proportional (√(ab)) with the simple average ((a+b)/2).
5) Not simplifying the ratio to lowest terms before comparing with another ratio.

IMPORTANT NOTES

• If a:b = c:d, then a:b:c:d relationships can be combined using compounding (multiplying corresponding ratios) when scales match.

• Ratios can be compared by converting to a common second term or by cross multiplication.

• Duplicate ratio of a:b is a²:b²; sub-duplicate ratio is √a:√b.

• Inverse (or reciprocal) ratio of a:b is 1/a : 1/b, equivalent to b:a.

formulas.title: Cross multiplication in proportion
formulas.content: If a:b = c:d, then a × d = b × c

formulas.title: Dividing a quantity in ratio
formulas.content: For Q divided in ratio a:b, parts = [a/(a+b)]×Q and [b/(a+b)]×Q

formulas.title: Mean proportional
formulas.content: Mean proportional between a and b = √(a × b)

formulas.title: Third proportional
formulas.content: Third proportional to a, b = b² / a

formulas.title: Fourth proportional
formulas.content: Fourth proportional to a, b, c = (b × c) / a

shortcutTips.title: Simplify first
shortcutTips.tip: Always reduce a ratio to lowest terms using HCF before performing further calculations — smaller numbers mean fewer errors.

shortcutTips.title: Assign a common multiplier
shortcutTips.tip: For a ratio a:b:c, represent quantities as ak, bk, ck and solve for k using any additional condition given.

shortcutTips.title: Use cross multiplication for proportions
shortcutTips.tip: Whenever you see a:b = c:d, immediately convert to a×d = b×c to solve for an unknown term.

shortcutTips.title: Mean proportional shortcut
shortcutTips.tip: Recall √(ab), not (a+b)/2, whenever a question asks for the 'mean proportional' between two numbers.

shortcutTips.title: Combine ratios via LCM
shortcutTips.tip: To combine a:b and b:c into a:b:c, scale each ratio so the common term (b) matches using LCM before merging.

solvedExamples.problem: Divide Rs 3,600 between A and B in the ratio 5:4.
solvedExamples.solution: A gets Rs 2,000 and B gets Rs 1,600.

solvedExamples.problem: Find the mean proportional between 4 and 25.
If a:b = 2:3 and b:c = 4:5, find a:b:c.
steps: a:b = 2:3 = 8:12 (multiplying both terms by 4 to match b's value with the next ratio).
b:c = 4:5 = 12:15 (multiplying both terms by 3 so b matches 12).

solvedExamples.problem: If (x+3):(x−3) = 5:3, find x using componendo and dividendo.
steps: Given a/b = c/d form with a=(x+3), b=(x−3), matching 5/3.

references.title: Khan Academy — Ratios, rates, and proportions
